title: Safety First ⚠️
description: Why the WHERE clause is your best friend.

slides.title: Catastrophe 💣
slides.content:
# The biggest mistake in SQL

What happens if you run this query?
`UPDATE users SET status = 'Pro';`

Because you didn't specify exactly *who* should get the update, the database faithfully updates EVERY SINGLE ROW IN THE ENTIRE TABLE.

You just accidentally gave 5 million users a free Pro account. You are fired.

slides.title: The WHERE Safety Net 🛟
slides.content:
## Targeting the update

You MUST almost always use a **`WHERE`** clause when running an `UPDATE` statement to limit the blast radius.

`UPDATE users SET status = 'Pro' WHERE id = 5;`

Now, only the single user with ID #5 is upgraded. Your job is safe.

quizData.question: What is the consequence of running an UPDATE statement without a WHERE clause?

options.text: It throws an automatic safety error and doesn't run
It alters every single row in the whole entire table simultaneously
It only alters the very first row it finds

quizData.explanation: If you don't restrict an UPDATE with a WHERE clause, it applies the SET command universally across every record.

slides.title: Using the Primary Key 🔑
slides.content:
## The safest target

When updating specific rows, you should always target their Primary Key (like `id`). 

If you try `WHERE first_name = 'John'`, you might accidentally update 50 different Johns at once! ID numbers are strictly unique, guaranteeing you only ever hit one target.

quizData.question: Why is it dangerous to run an UPDATE using a condition like `WHERE last_name = 'Smith'`?

options.text: Because it might unintentionally overwrite the data for hundreds of people who happen to share that last name
Because LAST_NAME isn't a valid column

quizData.explanation: Names aren't unique. To be surgically precise with your updates, always use the unique Primary Key.

slides.title: Safely Update!
slides.content: Update the `inventory` table. `SET` the `stock` to `0`, but ONLY `WHERE` the `id` is exactly `99`.
